coredata.dc:title: A magnetic sensing probe of zinc oxide decorated carbon foam and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots in a selective polymer for the determination of florfenicol in milk
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Seangyai, Jutarat
Chansud, Nutnicha
Bunkoed, Opas
coredata.dc:description: An optosensor based on fluorescence quenching was developed and utilized for the determination of florfenicol. The probe comprised nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), zinc oxide decorated carbon foam (ZnO*CF) and magnetite nanoparticles (Fe3O4) in a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). The fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe was emitted at 410 nm. The intensity of fluorescence emissions decreased linearly with increments of florfenicol from 0.50 to 100 µg L-1. The limit of detection was 0.17 µg L-1. The developed optosensor based on the nanocomposite probe was applied to determine florfenicol in milk samples. Recoveries from 90.5 % to 101.5 % were achieved with a relative standard deviation (RSD) lower than 3 %. The real-sample analysis results of the proposed method agreed well with the results of HPLC analysis. The developed optosensor showed good sensitivity and selectivity and could be applied for the determination of other substances in various samples by adjusting some measurement conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Florfenicol
Fluorescent probe
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Zinc oxide
Carbon foam
Optosensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
Synthesis of zinc oxide decorated carbon foam (ZnO*CF)
Characterization of the nanocomposite probe and related materials
Optimization of nanocomposite probe fabrication and determination condition
Effect of pH of dispersion solution
Mole ratio of template to monomer and cross linker
Effect of amount of ZnO*carbon foam
Comparison of various probes for the determination of florfenicol

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) and Prince of Songkla University (Grant No SCI6601083S ).

This research was supported by the National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) and Prince of Songkla University (Grant No SCI6601083S).The Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC). The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading. Jutarat Seangyai was supported by a Graduate Fellowship (Research Assistant) from the Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University (Contract no.1-2565-02-002).

The Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC). The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading. Jutarat Seangyai was supported by a Graduate Fellowship (Research Assistant) from the Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University (Contract no.1-2565-02-002).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The illustration outlines the synthesis of the proposed N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP fluorescent probe for the determination of florfenicol.
FTIR spectra of florfenicol (template molecule) (a), N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP before template removal (b), N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP after template removal (c), ZnO*CF (d) and Fe3O4 (e).

TEM images are of N-GQDs (a); inset is a particle size distribution and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP (b). SEM images are of ZnO*CF (c) and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP (d); inset is a particle size distribution. The adsorption and desorption isotherms are of ZnO*CF (e), N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/NIP (f) and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP (g).

Detection conditions and fabrication conditions of the proposed N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe were optimized. Chart (a) shows the effect on fluorescence quenching of binding time between florfenicol and the composite sensing probe. The other parameters were optimized by considering the sensitivity of the probe toward florfenicol, as determined from the slope of a calibration curve (0.50–100.0 μg L-1). Chart (b) shows the effect of dispersion solution pH on sensitivity. Chart (c) shows the effect on sensitivity of template–monomer–cross linker. Chart (d) shows the effect on sensitivity of ZnO*CF loading.
Sensitivity of different nanoprobes toward florfenicol.

The fluorescence spectra of the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP nanoprobe (a) and the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/NIP nanoprobe (b) in the presence of florfenicol at 0.50–100 μg L-1. The calibration curves (c) are of N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP (blue line) and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/NIP (red line). The photograph (d) shows the proposed N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe in the absence (left) and presence of florfenicol at the concentration of 100 µg L-1 (right) (under UV light).
Chart (a) shows the selectivity of the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe. A competitive study (b) was carried out using thiamphenicol and florfenicol at different concentrations.
The stability (a) and reproducibility (b) of the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP sensing probe for the detection of florfenicol.

Chart (a) shows the agreement between the developed optosensor and HPLC for the determination of florfenicol in milk. The HPLC chromatogram (b) shows the results of a spike and recovery test using 0.0, 1.0, 10.0, 50.0 and 100.0 µg L-1 in milk.
The nanocomposite N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe (n = 3) was used to determine florfenicol in milk. The results include a spike and recovery test at four concentrations.
Analytical methods for the determination of florfenicol in various samples.

head.ce:title: A magnetic sensing probe of zinc oxide decorated carbon foam and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots in a selective polymer for the determination of florfenicol in milk

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An optosensor based on fluorescence quenching was developed and utilized for the determination of florfenicol. The probe comprised nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), zinc oxide decorated carbon foam (ZnO*CF) and magnetite nanoparticles (Fe3O4) in a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). The fluorescence intensity of the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe was emitted at 410 nm. The intensity of fluorescence emissions decreased linearly with increments of florfenicol from 0.50 to 100 µg L-1. The limit of detection was 0.17 µg L-1. The developed optosensor based on the nanocomposite probe was applied to determine florfenicol in milk samples. Recoveries from 90.5 % to 101.5 % were achieved with a relative standard deviation (RSD) lower than 3 %. The real-sample analysis results of the proposed method agreed well with the results of HPLC analysis. The developed optosensor showed good sensitivity and selectivity and could be applied for the determination of other substances in various samples by adjusting some measurement conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Florfenicol is an antibiotic belonging to the phenicol family. It is used to prevent and treat bacterial diseases of livestock and aquatic animals (Barreto et al. 2016) but can leave residual traces in food such as eggs, milk and meat (Summa et al. 2015). The European Union (EU) has applied maximum residue limits (MRL) of florfenicol in foodstuffs and MRLs have been established for muscle, skin, liver, and kidney. To avoid potentially detrimental effects on consumers, a reliable method for the determination of florfenicol in food must be developed (Caro et al. 2020). Florfenicol can be detected by liquid chromatography (Fedeniuk et al. 2015; Imran et al. 2017; Xie et al. 2011), ELISA (Li et al. 2018), micellar electrokinetic chromatographic (Hillaert and Bossche, 2004) and electrochemiluminescence immunoassay (Ma et al. 2021), but all these techniques have limitations or drawbacks. The analysis may take a long time, or entail a complex sample preparation or the use of costly equipment. These problems can be avoided by the use of spectrofluorimetry, which is a rapid, simple and cost-effective method that can integrate a highly sensitive nanocomposite probe to improve analytical performances.

Highly sensitive fluorescent probes can be achieved with the use of quantum dots (QDs). QDs have good fluorescent properties and provide advantages including broad excitation ranges, narrow emission ranges and symmetrical emission spectra (Wang and Chen, 2011). Carbon-based quantum dots and semiconductor quantum dots (Chansud and Bunkoed, 2021) have both found applications but graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have some superior features. Their toxicity is low, dispersibility excellent, and they are biocompatible (Tsai et al. 2020). The quantum efficiency of GQDs can be enhanced by doping with heteroatoms to obtain nitrogen-doped GQDs, which possess superior fluorescence properties for spectrofluorimetry (Zhang et al. 2020).

The selectivity of a sensor must also be considered when determining trace organic compounds in complex samples. Selectivity and specificity can be improved by integrating N-GQDs with a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). MIPs are highly selective toward target analytes (Xiong et al. 2022). The recognition sites of an MIP complement the analyte in terms of shape, size and functional groups (Li et al. 2021; Xiao et al. 2018). Nanocomposite sensing probes based on N-GQDs incorporated in an MIP have been used as optosensor for the detection of tetracyclines (Zhang et al. 2020), warfarin (Li et al. 2022), thiacloprid (Liu et al. 2018) and chloramphenicol (Tsai et al. 2020). Furthermore, the mass transfer of the analyte to binding sites on MIP can be increased if affinity adsorption can be promoted. For the determination of trace florfenicol, ZnO*carbon foam (ZnO*CF) is an interesting adsorption material (Sajid et al. 2016) that can adsorb florfenicol through hydrogen bonding even though residual florfenicol concentrations are normally low, and milk frequently contain many interferences. Combining magnetite in the nanoprobe can isolate the nanoprobe from the washing solution resulting in convenient operation.

In this work, a nanoprobe was developed for the determination of florfenicol by integrating of N-GQDs as a fluorescence sensing material, the selectivity of MIP, the good adsorptive properties of ZnO*CF to enhance adsorption ability and magnetite nanoparticles (N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP). An optosensor based on the nanocomposite probe was applied to detect florfenicol in milk and also compared with a high-performance liquid chromatography method (HPLC) and its advantages over HPLC method were demonstrated.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, ≥99 %) and florfenicol were from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). Citric acid, 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES, ≥98 %), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl30.6 H2O), di-sodium hydrogen orthophosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO40.12 H2O), iron (II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl20.4 H2O), tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris-HMA), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)20.6 H2O), sucrose (C12H22O11) and sodium dihydrogen orthophosphate (NaH2PO40.2 H2O) were purchased from Univar (Ajax Finechem, Australia). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ammonia (NH3) and ethanol (EtOH) were from Labscan (Bangkok, Thailand).

Fluorescence measurements were performed by Cary eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-2600i UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The morphologies of the fluorescent probe and related materials were studied using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Apreo FEI, Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared spectra were obtained using the BX FTIR spectroscope (PerkinElmer, MA, USA).

The determination of florfenicol by HPLC was performed using the 1100 series HPLC (Agilent Technologies, Germany). The analytical column was a Fortis C18 (5 µm, 4.6 mm × 15 cm) using a mobile phase of ultrapure water and acetonitrile 60:40 (v/v) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min-1 and the sample injection volume was 20 µL. Florfenicol was detected with a diode array detector (DAD) at a wavelength of 223 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs were prepared via pyrolysis of citric acid with Tris-HMA according to a previous method with minor modification (Lin et al. 2015). In a round-bottom flask, 2.00 g of citric acid and 0.50 g of Tris-HMA were mixed and heated with a heating mantle at 225 °C for 15 min, until it became an orange solid. Then, the obtained solid product was placed in a beaker and 10 mL of DI water were added. The obtained solution was neutralized to pH 7 by adding 0.25 M NaOH dropwise. The neutralized solution containing synthesized N-GQDs was then transferred into a 50 mL centrifuge tube and stored at 4 °C until used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of zinc oxide decorated carbon foam (ZnO*CF)
ce:section.ce:para: ZnO*CF was synthesized according to the following procedure. First, 3.00 g of sucrose and 1.50 g of Zn(NO3)20.6 H2O were mixed and heated to 110 °C. As the temperature rose, the melting mixture was stirred continuously with a glass rod. At 105 °C, the mixture turned yellow and the foam started to expand. After heating for about 10 min at 110 °C, the color of the foam started to change from yellow to black. ZnO*CF was obtained and kept in a desiccator.

Fe3O4 were prepared by co-precipitating a mixture of FeCl30.6 H2O and FeCl20.4 H2O as described in a recent study (Bunkoed et al. 2020). First, 1.13 g of FeCl20.4 H2O and 3.00 g of FeCl30.6 H2O were dissolved in 150 mL of DI water. The obtained solution was heated to 70 °C under vigorous agitation to obtain a homogenous solution. Ammonia solution was added and the mixture was stirred for 1 h at 80 °C under a nitrogen shielding gas. Black Fe3O4 precipitates were collected with an external magnet and, after cooling to ambient temperature, were washed three times in 50 mL of DI water. Finally, the Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dried for 6 h at 80 °C.

The N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP fluorescent probe was synthesized according to the following procedure ( Fig. 1). First, 9.00 mg of florfenicol, 5.00 mL of DI water and 5.00 mL of N-GQDs were mixed and stirred at 500 rpm for 30 min. Next, 180 µL of APTES was added as a functional monomer, and stirring continued at 500 rpm for 1 h. Then, 5.0 mg of Fe3O4, 6.0 mg of ZnO*CF, 87 µL of TEOS (cross linker) and 200 µL of NH3 were added into the mixture, and stirring continued for 1.5 h. Finally, the florfenicol was removed by washing twice in 20 mL of ethanol with sonication. The magnetic nanoprobe was simply isolated from washing solution using an external magnet. An N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/NIP probe was also synthesized using the same strategy but without the florfenicol.

The N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/NIP probes were dispersed separately in phosphate buffer at pH 7, and 300 µL were mixed with 100 µL of florfenicol standard or sample and incubated for 22 min at 25 °C. Fluorescence emission was then measured from 360 to 700 nm, with the excitation wavelength set at 330 nm and a slit width of 10 nm.

Milk samples were pretreated according to the following procedure (Chullasat et al. 2019). A 10.0 mL sample of milk was placed in a 50 mL centrifuge tube and spun at 5000 rpm for 20 min to remove fat. Then, 10.0 mL of acetonitrile were added to the defatted milk, vortexed for 1 min and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was separated and evaporated at 60 °C. The residue was re-dissolved with 10.0 mL of DI water and measured with the developed optosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the nanocomposite probe and related materials
ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups of the nanocomposite probes were investigated using FTIR spectroscopy. The spectrum of florfenicol which is used as template molecule showed absorption bands at 1686 and 1535 cm-1, which were assigned to the carbonyl stretching vibration and C-N stretching vibration, respectively. The bands at 3454 and 3321 cm-1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of -NH and -OH ( Fig. 2 (a)). The characteristic peaks of N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP before template removal (Fig. 2 (b)), at 1604 and 591 cm-1, were attributed to the CC bond in ZnO*CF and Fe-O stretching vibration. After template removal (Fig. 2 (c)), peaks at 1057 and 776 cm-1 corresponded to Si-O-Si and Si-O stretching. The FTIR spectrum of ZnO*CF (Fig. 2 (d)) showed absorption peaks at 1604 and 3406 cm-1 that respectively indicated the stretching vibration of CC and hydroxyl groups. The spectrum of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 2 (e)) showed characteristic peaks at 591, 1623 and 3422 cm-1 that were attributed to Fe-O stretching, the bending vibration of water and -OH stretching, respectively. These results confirmed that ZnO*CF and Fe3O4 were composited with the MIP formed by the polymerization of APTES and TEOS.

The morphologies of N-GQDs and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP were characterized using TEM. The TEM images of N-GQDs ( Fig. 3 a) and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP (Fig. 3 b) showed uniform spherical particles. The morphologies of ZnO*CF and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP were characterized using SEM. The SEM images revealed the foam-like structure of ZnO*CF (Fig. 3 c) and the rough surface of N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP (Fig. 3 d). The average particle size of N-GQDs and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP were about 4 nm and 300 nm, respectively.

The quantum yields of N-GQDs, N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP and N-GQDs-MIP were determined using quinine hemisulfate salt monohydrate as a reference. The quantum yields were calculated according to the following equation.
∅
x
=
∅
ST
×
Grad
x
Grad
ST
×
η
x
η
ST
2
Where
∅
x
and
∅
ST
are the quantum yields of the target compound and quinine standard, respectively. The quantum yield of quinine standard is 54 %.
Grad
x
and
Grad
ST
are the gradient from the linear regression analysis of the target compound and quinine standard, respectively.
η
x
and
η
ST
are the solvent refractive index of target compound and quinine standard. The refractive index of water is 1.33.

The respective yields were 0.55, 0.74 and 0.71. The BET surface areas of ZnO*CF and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/NIP and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP 1.669, 6.296 were 6.433 m2 g-1, respectively. The adsorption and desorption isotherms of ZnO*CF and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/NIP and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP are showed in Fig. 3 e, Fig. 3 f and Fig. 3 g, respectively. The results indicated that specific recognitions cavities were produced that could bind with the target analyte.

Before applying the fabricated nanoprobe to determine florfenicol in real samples, the complete removal of the template was confirmed to avoid any memory effect that could affect the accuracy of the detection system. The emission spectrum of the probe before removal of the template indicated low intensity (Fig. S1a) but after removal of the template (Fig. S1b), intensity was restored to the same level exhibited by the NIP probe (Fig. S1c). These results indicated that the template was completely removed and that the probe was able to bind with the analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of nanocomposite probe fabrication and determination condition
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the best sensing performance for the determination of florfenicol, several affecting parameters were investigated, including binding time, dispersion solution pH, the mole ratio of template to monomer and cross linker, and the loading of ZnO*CF. The optimal binding time was determined by considering the quenching of fluorescence intensity (F0/F). The other parameters were optimized by considering sensitivity determined from the slope of a calibration curve performed in the concentration range of 0.50–100.0 μg L-1.

The effect of binding time between florfenicol and the probe was studied. Fluorescence quenching increased from 0 to 22 min and then remained relatively constant ( Fig. 4 a). Thus, the optimal binding time between florfenicol and the probe was 22 min

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH of dispersion solution
ce:section.ce:para: The acidity or basicity of the medium could impact the binding efficiency of the probe. The pH of the phosphate buffer solution was varied from 6.0 to 9.0. The best sensitivity was achieved at pH 7.0 (Fig. 4 b). Sensitivity was lower in the acidic condition because APTES decomposed as -NH2 groups in the recognition cavities underwent protonation, which reduced hydrogen bonding between APTES and florfenicol. In the basic condition, sensitivity was lower due to the ionization of the silica layer. Consequently, pH 7.0 was selected as the appropriate condition of the dispersion solution for the detection of florfenicol by the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mole ratio of template to monomer and cross linker
ce:section.ce:para: The mole ratio of template to monomer (APTES) and cross linker (TEOS) is an important factor in the detection of the analyte since different ratios produce probes with different physical and chemical characteristics. The template proportion was fixed at 1, the proportion of APTES was varied at 25, 30 and 35, and the proportion of TEOS was varied at 10, 15 and 20. The highest sensitivity for the determination of florfenicol was achieved at the template–monomer–cross linker mole ratio of 1:30:15 (Fig. 4 c). At the lower APTES proportion, sensitivity was lower because there were fewer functional groups present during polymerization, and fewer recognition sites could be formed on the surface of the probe. Sensitivity was also lower at the higher proportion of APTES, perhaps because excess monomer interfered in the formation of recognition sites. Sensitivity was lower at the lower proportion of TEOS because the MIP layer was unstable, and at the higher proportion of TEOS, sensitivity was lower due to excess cross linker disturbed the binding of template and monomer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of amount of ZnO*carbon foam
ce:section.ce:para: The amount of ZnO*CF in the composite could impact the sensitivity of the nanoprobe toward florfenicol. The effect of ZnO*CF loading was studied at 4.0, 5.0, 6.0 and 7.0 mg. The results showed that 6.0 mg provided the highest sensitivity (Fig. 4 d). Sensitivity was lower at the higher loading because excess ZnO*CF could interfere with the formation of recognition sites. Sensitivity was also lower at the lower loadings due to the insufficient number of adsorption sites for the analyte. Thus, the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe was prepared using 6.0 mg of ZnO*CF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of various probes for the determination of florfenicol
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent probes of different nanocomposites were synthesized and investigated for the determination of florfenicol. The nanocomposites included N-GQDs/Fe3O4/NIP, N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/NIP, N-GQDs/Fe3O4/MIP and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP. The highest sensitivity was exhibited by the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP nanoprobe ( Fig. 5). The superior sensitivity of the probe was due to the recognition sites on the surface of MIP and the presence of ZnO*CF that helped enhance affinity adsorption between the probe and florfenicol. The N-GQDs/Fe3O4/NIP probe showed the lowest sensitivity since there were no specific recognition sites nor affinity material for florfenicol adsorption.

The performances of the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP and N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/NIP nanoprobes were assessed by determining different concentrations of florfenicol in the optimized condition. The fluorescence emission spectra of N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe decreased linearly with increasing florfenicol concentration (
Fig. 6 a). Whereas the fluorescence emission spectra N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/NIP probe decreased only slightly ( Fig. 6 b). The quenching of the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe was more significant because the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe presented recognition sites for florfenicol binding.

The fluorescence quenching of the developed optosensor provided a good linearity from 0.50 to 100 μg L-1 with a R2 greater than 0.995 (Fig. 6 c). The limit of detection, based on 3 SD/slope, was 0.17 μg L-1, where SD is the standard deviation of the blank response. Fig. 6 d shows a photograph of the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP fluorescent probe in the absence and presence of florfenicol.

The quenching mechanism of the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP can be explained by the Stern-Volmer equation,
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
[ C ]
, where
F
0
and F are the intensities of the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP in the absence and presence of florfenicol; Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant, and
[ C ]
is the concentration of florfenicol. The quenching of the probe by florfenicol is possible by electron transfer when florfenicol rebinds with the nanoprobe. In addition, the UV–vis spectrum of florfenicol and the emission spectrum of the probe did not overlap (Fig. S2), therefore, resonance energy transfer is not possible quenching mechanism (Xu et al. 2022).

The selectivity of the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe was evaluated by determining analog structures of florfenicol. The sensitivity of the probe toward chloramphenicol, thiamphenicol, sulfamerazine, sulfamethazine, sulfadimethoxine and sulfadiazine was lower than sensitivity toward florfenicol ( Fig. 7 a) due to structural differences between the analogs and florfenicol. These differences limited the binding between recognition cavities of the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe and the analog structures.

A competitive study of binding was performed using the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe and the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/NIP probe. The study measured sensitivity of the probe in the presence of various concentrations ratio of thiamphenicol to florfenicol. Sensitivity did not change with increments of thiamphenicol (Fig. 7 b). The results demonstrated that the probe was highly specific for florfenicol and could be used to determine florfenicol in food samples.

The stability of the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP fluorescent probe was evaluated in buffer solution at pH 7.0 ( Fig. 8 a). The nanoprobe exhibited good stability. Fluorescence intensity did not change over 450 min, which is enough time to measure the florfenicol in real samples.

The reproducibility of the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP probe was evaluated by fabricating six probes at different times in the optimum condition. An acceptable RSD of 1.06 % was achieved (Fig. 8 b). The results demonstrated the good reproducibility of the probe preparation.

The developed optosensor was compared with HPLC and the results were in good agreement, with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.9975 ( Fig. 9 a). An HPLC chromatogram of spiked milk samples is shown in Fig. 9 b. These results confirmed the accuracy of the proposed optosensor for analysis of florfenicol and the method has some advantages over HPLC since it does not require a harmful organic solvent and the cost of analysis is lower. Moreover, the developed optosensor is highly sensitive and selective toward florfenicol and provides fast results. The analytical performance of the developed optosensor using the N-GQDs/ZnO*CF/Fe3O4/MIP fluorescent probe was also compared with other previous reports as summarized in Table 2. The developed method exhibited a wide linearity with low limit of detection. The recoveries of the present method were better than or similar to other works. The repeatability of the developed optosensor was also satisfactory. Moreover, this developed optosensor is simple and rapid to operate. In addition, the equipment costs much cheaper than equipment for chromatographic techniques.

An optosensing probe was developed for the determination of florfenicol. The composite nanoprobe consisted of N-doped GQDs, zinc oxide decorated carbon foam and Fe3O4 embedded in selective polymer. Integrating these materials produced a highly selective and sensitive probe, allowing florfenicol measurement at trace levels. The optosensor was applied for the determination of florfenicol in milk, obtaining recoveries from 90.5 % to 101.5 %. This optosensor provides a highly sensitive and accurate method for the determination of trace florfenicol in complex sample matrices.
This article does not contain any studies with human and animal subjects.
The authors declare that they have no competing interests.
This research was supported by the National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) and Prince of Songkla University (Grant No SCI6601083S).

Jutarat Seangyai: Methodology, Formal analysis, Software, Validation, Writing – Original Draft. Nutnicha Chansud: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – Review & Editing. Opas Bunkoed: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data Curation, Review & Editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC). The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading. Jutarat Seangyai was supported by a Graduate Fellowship (Research Assistant) from the Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University (Contract no.1-2565-02-002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2023.105545.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
